3 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

If you have spent any time looking into global state management in React, you have likely run into a confusing web of names: Redux, React Redux, and Redux Toolkit (RTK).
If you are confused about how they differ and which one you should actually use, do not worry. Let’s break down the confusion and analyze the technical differences.
Setting the Record Straight: What’s in a Name?
Before looking at code, let’s clear up a major misconception that trips up almost every beginner: React Redux and Redux Toolkit are not rivals. They actually work together.

• Core Redux (Vanilla Redux): This is the standalone state management library. It is completely independent of React and holds your global application memory.

• React Redux: This is the bridge or connector library. It provides hooks like useSelector and useDispatch so your React components can talk to your Redux store.

• Redux Toolkit (RTK): This is the official, modern way to write Redux logic. It acts as an updated wrapper around core Redux, bundled with tools to eliminate repetitive setup code.
When people say “React Redux vs. Redux Toolkit,” what they usually mean is “Legacy, hand-written Redux vs. Modern Redux Toolkit.” Today, you use Redux Toolkit to write your logic, and React Redux to connect it to your UI.
The Problem: Why Everyone Hated Legacy Redux
In the early days of web development, classic Redux was notorious for driving beginners away. Writing even a simple feature required you to touch four or five different files and write an overwhelming amount of repetitive code called boilerplate.
To update a simple user profile in legacy Redux, you had to:

• Define a unique action type string constant ('SET_USER_NAME').

• Write an action creator function that returns that type and payload.

• Write a massive reducer function with a switch statement.

• Carefully perform an immutable update using object spreads (...state) so you didn't accidentally mutate (change) the original state directly.

• Manually configure your store, download separate packages for asynchronous network requests (redux-thunk), and hook up the DevTools extension.
It felt like assembling a spaceship just to go down the street.
The Solution: Enter Redux Toolkit (RTK)
Redux Toolkit was built by the Redux team to solve these frustrations. Think of it as Redux with automated guardrails. It addresses the main pain points through two major technical changes:
1. The Power of createSlice
Instead of splitting actions and reducers across separate files, RTK introduces a single function called createSlice. You give it a name, an initial state, and an object full of reducer functions. RTK automatically generates your action types and action creators behind the scenes.
2. Mutation Safety via Immer
In classic Redux, accidentally mutating your state caused quiet UI bugs:
// ✗ BAD: Legacy Redux mutation (breaks React re-renders)
state.user.name = "Alex";
return state;Redux Toolkit integrates a library called Immer. Immer lets you write intuitive, “mutating” code, but intercepts it safely under the hood to ensure perfect immutability.
// ✓ GOOD: Redux Toolkit (Immer safely converts this under the hood)
state.user.name = "Alex";Head-to-Head Comparison

• BoilerPlate: High and requires manual action creators, constants, and switch blocks with Vanilla Redux but Minimal in RTK as actions and reducers are generated dynamically via Slices.

• State Updates: Vanilla Redux requires manual immutable copying ({ ...state, data: newValue }). RTK directly assign values (state.data = newValue) via Immer.

• Async Tasks: Vanilla Redux requires installing and configuring separate middleware like redux-thunk. In RTK, native async handling is built-in via createAsyncThunk or RTK Query.

• Store Setup: In Vanilla Redux, complex configuration is required for DevTools, middleware, and logging. But in RTK, a single function call (configureStore) sets up defaults automatically.
So, in conclusion, RTK is the trend hereafter. While legacy apps might still follow Vanilla Redux, newer developed apps should be using the advantages of RTK as its easier to read, write and interpret from a developer’s perspective. That makes the development experience more simpler, cleaner and efficient.
